question: You went along with the group even though you knew it was a bad idea. It went wrong exactly like you thought it would. Someone asks whose idea it was. What do you do?
option_a: Say it was not your idea since you never wanted to do it
option_b: Stay quiet and let someone else speak
option_c: Say honestly that you had doubts but went along with it anyway
option_d: Blame the person who started it
explanation: This comes up in group projects, in dares, in plans that go wrong on the way home. Being honest that you went along even when you had doubts is harder than blaming someone else -- but it is the thing that builds real trust.
hint: What is the honest thing to say even when it is uncomfortable?

question: You said you needed more time to think. Now someone is saying you always do this and why can you never just go with things. How do you answer?
option_a: Say sorry and agree with them
option_b: Say nothing and go quiet
option_c: Say calmly that you just want to make sure it is a good idea before you say yes
option_d: Walk away from the group
explanation: This follow-up pressure happens in classrooms, friendship groups, sports teams -- anywhere one person slows down to think and the group gets impatient. Always and never are words used to make you feel like the problem. One calm sentence is enough. You are thinking. That is allowed.
hint: Does someone saying you always do something make it true?

question: You told the group you did not want to climb through the building site fence and walked off. Now on Monday someone says you were scared. What do you do?
option_a: Explain to everyone why it was dangerous
option_b: Say nothing -- people who know you will know it is not true
option_c: Agree that yes you were scared just to end it
option_d: Tell a teacher what they are saying
explanation: This happens after any moment where you made a sensible choice and someone wants to make it look weak. You made a good decision. Someone putting a label on it does not change that. The people who know you already know the truth.
hint: Does a label someone gives you have to stick?

question: You stayed friendly with the person the group was ignoring. Now someone is saying you are taking sides. Are you?
option_a: Yes -- being friendly with someone is taking sides
option_b: No -- being decent to someone is not the same as taking sides against everyone else
option_c: Maybe -- you should have thought about that before
option_d: Yes -- and you should apologise to the group
explanation: Being kind to one person does not mean you are against everyone else. Anyone saying it does is trying to force you to choose. In most cases you do not have to -- and the person saying it knows that too.
hint: Is being kind to someone the same as being against everyone else?

question: Last week the Primary 7s kicked your group off the far patch mid-game. Today everyone wants to play there again. Mia says are we sure about that? Everyone tells her to stop worrying. You are the one the group listens to. What do you do?
option_a: Go with the majority -- most people want the far patch
option_b: Say look it happened last week -- let's just use the other side today
option_c: Tell Mia she needs to just go with it
option_d: Say nothing -- it is not your job
explanation: This happens in any group heading somewhere that caused a problem before. You were there. You know. Saying it simply and offering a direction forward is all it takes. The group follows because you moved first.
hint: Does remembering what happened before help make a better decision now?

question: Your group is being horrible about someone who is not there. It is getting a bit much. Everyone is laughing. What do you do?
option_a: Join in -- it is just a laugh
option_b: Go quiet but say nothing
option_c: Say something like alright that is enough and change the subject
option_d: Tell the group they are being horrible
explanation: This comes up in classrooms, group chats, changing rooms after PE, walking home. You do not need a speech. Saying alright that is enough and moving the conversation on is quick, not preachy, and it changes the energy in the room.
hint: Does changing the subject need a big explanation?

question: You suggested something for the group project. Jamie said nah boring and moved on. Two minutes later Jamie said basically the same thing and everyone loved it. You are the one people listen to. What do you do?
option_a: Say nothing -- at least it is a good idea
option_b: Say out loud hang on -- did they not just say that?
option_c: Tell Jamie off after the lesson
option_d: Vote for a different idea just to make a point
explanation: This happens in group projects, team meetings, any group where one voice gets heard more. You have the influence to name what just happened without turning it into a fight. Saying it simply means the right person gets the credit.
hint: Is it fair to let something like that go without saying anything?

question: Your group is about to do something you are pretty sure will get everyone in trouble. You can see it clearly. Nobody else seems bothered. What is the hardest but best thing to do?
option_a: Go along with it -- if everyone gets in trouble at least you are together
option_b: Say nothing but do not join in
option_c: Say clearly this is going to go wrong and suggest something else
option_d: Wait until it goes wrong then say you knew it would
explanation: This comes up on the way home from school, in group dares, in plans that seem fine to everyone else but you can already see where they lead. Offering something else at the same time as saying it is wrong makes it easier for the group to turn around without feeling embarrassed.
hint: Is it better to say something before it goes wrong or after?

question: You said the group's plan was going to go wrong. It did not -- it worked out fine. Now someone says you are always so negative. How do you handle that?
option_a: Apologise for saying it
option_b: Say you are glad it worked out and leave it there
option_c: Explain all the reasons you thought it would go wrong
option_d: Say well it might have gone wrong
explanation: This happens any time you raise a concern and it turns out fine. You were not wrong to say it. It is good that it worked out. Being genuinely glad about that and moving on is more powerful than defending yourself.
hint: Do you need to win an argument to have been right to say something?

question: You changed the group's direction and it worked. Now the person who wanted to do it the original way is being off with you. What do you do?
option_a: Apologise even though the decision was right
option_b: Give them a bit of space and check in with them later
option_c: Tell the group what they are doing
option_d: Be extra friendly so they have to come round
explanation: This comes up on sports teams, in friendship groups, anywhere your decision went against what someone else wanted. Someone being off when a decision went against them is normal. A bit of space and a quiet check-in later is nearly always enough.
hint: Does a good decision mean everyone will be happy straight away?

question: You are chosen to read out loud in class. Your voice goes wobbly even though you know the words perfectly. What is happening?
option_a: You are bad at reading
option_b: Your body got a surprise and is catching up -- it happens to everyone
option_c: You should have practised more
option_d: You are showing off
explanation: This happens in class, at a sports presentation, reading something out at assembly. Your voice going wobbly when people are watching is your body reacting to being seen. It is not about how well you can read. It settles down the more you do it.
hint: Is your wobbly voice about reading or about being watched?

question: You fell out with your best friend on Monday. By Wednesday you have both sort of moved on but something still feels a bit off. What is that feeling?
option_a: Proof the friendship is over
option_b: Your feelings catching up with what your head already decided -- they take a bit longer
option_c: Nothing -- just ignore it
option_d: A sign you should bring the argument back up
explanation: This leftover feeling happens after any kind of falling out -- with a friend, on your team, even after a telling off that has been sorted. Your head moves on first and your feelings follow a bit later. The feeling usually fades on its own.
hint: Can your head and your feelings be in different places at the same time?

question: Something happened at home this morning and now you are at school and you cannot concentrate. You feel like you are watching the lesson through glass. What should you do?
option_a: Push through and pretend everything is fine
option_b: Tell a trusted adult you are having a hard morning
option_c: Cause a distraction so nobody notices
option_d: Go to the toilet and stay there
explanation: Things from outside follow us in -- to school, to training, to anywhere we are supposed to be focused. You do not have to explain everything. Telling one trusted person takes ten seconds and means someone knows -- which usually makes the rest of the day easier.
hint: Does something that happened outside school stay outside when you walk in?

question: Everyone in your group picks the same answer on a quiz. You picked something different. You are not sure if you are right or just different. What do you do?
option_a: Change your answer to match the group
option_b: Keep your answer -- you picked it for a reason
option_c: Ask the teacher to check before you decide
option_d: Cross out your answer so nobody sees
explanation: This happens on quizzes, in group decisions, any time one person sees something differently from everyone else. Everyone else picking the same thing does not make it right. Keep your answer until you actually know it is wrong -- not just because you are outnumbered.
hint: Is everyone picking the same thing the same as everyone being right?

question: Your group is laughing at the way someone in another group answered a question. You thought the answer was actually pretty good. What do you do?
option_a: Laugh along so you fit in
option_b: Stay quiet and keep your face straight
option_c: Say actually I thought that was a decent answer
option_d: Ask why everyone is laughing
explanation: This comes up in classrooms, watching someone perform, seeing someone try something new. One short sentence -- actually I thought that was decent -- is enough to stop a pile-on. Chances are you are not the only one in the group thinking it.
hint: Does saying what you actually think always have to be a big deal?

question: You kept your different answer on the quiz. You were right. Now the people who copied each other got it wrong. Someone says you only got it right by luck. What do you say?
option_a: Say nothing -- being right is enough
option_b: Say maybe -- but I kept my answer because I thought it through
option_c: Explain in detail how you worked it out
option_d: Say well you should have thought for yourself then
explanation: This comes after any moment where you went your own way and it paid off. You do not need to prove anything. Saying simply that you thought it through is calm and true. The result already did the talking.
hint: Do you need to prove yourself when the result already shows it?

question: Your group cannot agree on what game to play. Everyone is getting a bit grumpy. Nobody is deciding. You have got an idea. What do you do?
option_a: Wait for someone else to decide
option_b: Say right -- what about this and suggest something specific
option_c: Take a vote and make it official
option_d: Give up and play on your own
explanation: Groups get stuck like this on the playground, on sports teams, in any situation where nobody wants to go first. When a group is going in circles one person saying something specific moves things forward. That is not being bossy -- it is being useful.
hint: Is suggesting something the same as taking over?

question: You got picked for the team but your friend did not. You feel guilty even though it is not your fault. Is that feeling making sense?
option_a: No -- you did nothing wrong so you should not feel anything
option_b: Yes -- feeling bad when something good happens to you but not your friend is completely normal
option_c: No -- you should just feel happy
option_d: Yes -- you should tell the teacher to swap you out
explanation: This comes up whenever luck splits a group -- one person gets picked and another does not, one person gets invited and another does not. Feeling guilty when something goes your way and not your friend's means you care about them. You do not need to fix it -- just let it be there.
hint: Can you feel two things at the same time about the same moment?

question: Someone in your group has been left out all week. Nobody has said anything but everyone knows. The group keeps pretending it is not happening. What do you do?
option_a: Keep pretending along with everyone else
option_b: Go and sit with that person at lunch without making a big deal of it
option_c: Bring it up in front of the whole group
option_d: Tell a teacher everything
explanation: This happens in classrooms, on sports teams, in any group where someone has quietly been pushed to the edge. Going and sitting with someone without making a performance of it is the most useful single thing you can do and does not require anyone else to do anything.
hint: Does fixing a big situation always need a big action?

question: You can see the group is leaving someone out and it is getting worse every day. You are the one people follow. What one thing would change it fastest?
option_a: Have a serious talk with the group about being kind
option_b: Just include that person yourself -- the group will follow your lead
option_c: Write a note to the teacher
option_d: Wait -- it will sort itself out
explanation: This works the same way in any group. When the person the group looks to includes someone, the group adjusts around that without needing a big conversation. You do not need a speech. You just need to do it once and mean it.
hint: Does the person the group follows need to say something or just do something?

question: You stuck up for someone the group was leaving out. Now someone says you only did it to look good. What do you say?
option_a: Explain all your reasons for doing it
option_b: Say nothing -- you know why you did it
option_c: Say maybe -- but they still needed someone to sit with them
option_d: Ask why it matters why you did it
explanation: This challenge comes after any moment where you did something decent and someone questions your reason. Whether you did it to look good or not the person still needed someone. Saying so calmly ends the conversation. The reason does not change what actually happened.
hint: Does the reason for doing something kind change whether it was the right thing to do?

question: You are at the dinner table and the salt is out of reach. What do you do?
option_a: Reach across someone to get it
option_b: Say oi -- pass the salt
option_c: Say excuse me, could you pass the salt please
option_d: Just go without it
explanation: Asking politely for something small is the same habit as asking politely for something big. It also means the person you are asking feels respected rather than ordered around.
hint: Is how you ask for small things the same habit as how you ask for big things?

question: Someone gives you a birthday present you already have. What do you do?
option_a: Tell them straight away you already have it
option_b: Say nothing and look disappointed
option_c: Say thank you and mean it -- they thought about you
option_d: Ask if you can swap it
explanation: The gift is not the point -- the fact someone thought about you and made an effort is. Saying thank you genuinely is how you acknowledge that.
hint: Is saying thank you about the present or the person?

question: Your group is being loud in a queue and people around you look annoyed. Nobody else in your group notices. You do. What do you do?
option_a: Ignore it -- everyone else is doing it
option_b: Quietly say to the group -- let's keep it down a bit
option_c: Shout at your group to be quiet
option_d: Wait for someone else to say something
explanation: Reading a room and adjusting without being told is a social skill most adults still have not fully learned. Doing it quietly without making it a big thing is the smoothest way.
hint: Does adjusting your behaviour for the space you are in matter?

question: You are in a group chat and someone posts something that is clearly meant to be private. Everyone is screenshotting it. What do you do?
option_a: Screenshot it too -- everyone else is
option_b: Leave it -- it is not your business
option_c: Tell the person their message is being shared
option_d: Screenshot it but promise not to share
explanation: Once something private is being passed around, the person who sent it is already in trouble. Telling them quickly gives them a chance to do something about it. That is the decent thing.
hint: Is joining in the same as causing the problem?

question: Your group is laughing at an impression of a teacher. You are the one they look to. The teacher walks round the corner. What do you do?
option_a: Keep laughing -- it is too late anyway
option_b: Go quiet and look at the floor
option_c: Say something like -- alright, moving on, and walk
option_d: Point out who started it
explanation: When you are the one people follow, how you react sets the tone. Moving on quickly and calmly without drama is the most effective way to end the moment without making it worse.
hint: Does how you react change what the group does next?

question: You have been inside on a screen all day and you feel groggy and flat. What is actually going on?
option_a: You are coming down with something
option_b: Your body needs movement, fresh air or food -- it is telling you
option_c: You are bored and need a better game
option_d: You are tired and should go to bed
explanation: Screens do not make you tired in the same way running does. The groggy flat feeling is your body asking for something different -- movement, air, water, food. It is a signal, not a problem.
hint: Is the flat feeling about the screen or about what your body needs?

question: You stayed up an hour later than usual and now it is morning and everything feels harder. Why?
option_a: One hour cannot make that much difference
option_b: Sleep builds up -- losing an hour regularly adds up fast and affects everything
option_c: You are just not a morning person
option_d: You need more practice getting up early
explanation: One hour less sleep does not sound like much. But it affects concentration, mood, reactions and memory. Done regularly it adds up. Your brain does most of its repair work at night.
hint: Does one hour of sleep actually matter?

question: You have been snappy with people all morning and you do not know why. What is the most useful first question to ask yourself?
option_a: Who made me like this
option_b: Have I eaten, slept, or drunk enough water today
option_c: Why is everyone annoying me
option_d: Is this just my personality
explanation: Hunger, tiredness and dehydration all make irritability worse before anything else is happening. Checking the basics first is not weakness -- it is self-awareness. Often fixing one small physical thing changes everything.
hint: Could something physical be behind a mood?

question: You are dreading something tomorrow and your stomach feels horrible tonight. What is happening?
option_a: You are probably getting sick
option_b: Your brain is sending a stress signal to your body -- they are connected
option_c: You ate too much
option_d: You are being dramatic
explanation: The stomach is sometimes called the second brain. Anxiety, dread and excitement all show up physically -- racing heart, tight chest, stomach feeling off. Your body is not broken. It is just responding to what your brain is thinking.
hint: Can a thought cause a physical feeling?

question: Your friend says they never need a routine -- they just do things when they feel like it. How does that tend to work out for most people?
option_a: Fine -- routines are for babies
option_b: It usually means important things get forgotten or done at the last minute
option_c: Great -- freedom is better
option_d: Perfectly -- some people are just better without structure
explanation: Some people genuinely need less structure than others. But most people find that small regular habits -- a time to eat, sleep, do homework -- mean the important things get done without a daily battle. The routine does the deciding so your brain does not have to.
hint: Does a routine make things easier or harder?

question: You have had a really hard week and someone asks how you are. You say fine. Why do people usually say fine even when they are not?
option_a: Because they are lying
option_b: Because it is quicker and they do not know if the other person actually wants to know
option_c: Because they are fine really
option_d: Because they do not want sympathy
explanation: Fine is a placeholder. It usually means I am functioning. It is not always a lie -- sometimes it is just easier. But noticing when you say fine and mean something else is useful self-awareness.
hint: Is saying fine the same as being fine?

question: You are the person in your group who everyone comes to with problems. It helps them but it is starting to drain you. What do you do?
option_a: Keep going -- they need you
option_b: Tell them all to sort their own problems out
option_c: Be honest with the people you trust that you are finding it a lot right now
option_d: Pretend you are busy so nobody asks
explanation: Being the person people lean on is a sign of trust. But you also have a limit. Saying honestly to someone close that you are finding it a lot is not rejecting people -- it is being real about what you can give.
hint: Can helping others too much cost you something?

question: You want to get better at football but you do not know where to start. The best first step is...
option_a: Watch more football on TV
option_b: Pick one specific thing to practise and do it for ten minutes today
option_c: Tell everyone you are going to be a professional
option_d: Wait until training to ask the coach
explanation: Wanting to improve is not the same as improving. Picking one specific small thing and doing it today -- not waiting for the right moment -- is how progress actually starts.
hint: Is deciding to get better the same as getting better?

question: You set yourself a goal and after three days you are already bored of it. What does that usually mean?
option_a: You are lazy
option_b: The goal was probably too big or too vague -- smaller and more specific usually sticks better
option_c: Goals never work
option_d: You should try harder
explanation: Goals that feel boring after three days are usually either too big to feel real or too vague to know if you are making progress. Breaking them down into something you can see changing day by day makes them easier to stick to.
hint: Is a boring goal a sign to quit or a sign to make it smaller?

question: You tried something, it did not work, and now you do not want to try again. That feeling is...
option_a: Proof it is not for you
option_b: Normal -- but it is not the same as it actually being too hard
option_c: A sign you should move on
option_d: Laziness
explanation: The feeling after something does not work is real and it is uncomfortable. But that feeling is not information about whether you can do the thing. It is information about how much you care. Most things worth doing have that feeling in the middle.
hint: Is not wanting to try again the same as not being able to?

question: Someone in your class finds something easy that you find really hard. What is the most useful way to think about that?
option_a: They are just smarter than you
option_b: They have practised more or their brain works differently for that thing -- neither means you cannot improve
option_c: You should give up that subject
option_d: It is not fair
explanation: People are not either good or bad at things. They are at different stages with different amounts of practice. Someone finding something easy now does not mean you cannot get there -- it means they are further along that particular path right now.
hint: Does someone being better at something now mean they always will be?

question: You are working toward a goal and a friend says it is a bit unrealistic. What do you do?
option_a: Immediately drop it -- they know best
option_b: Listen to what they mean, think about it honestly, then decide for yourself
option_c: Ignore them completely
option_d: Argue until they agree with you
explanation: Friends can sometimes see things you cannot. But they can also be wrong. Listening properly and then making your own decision is different from either ignoring them or immediately giving in. The goal is yours.
hint: Is someone else's opinion about your goal more important than your own?

question: You have been working on something for weeks and it is still not going well. The group is moving on. What do you do?
option_a: Give up -- if it was working it would have worked by now
option_b: Decide whether the goal still matters enough to keep going, or whether it makes sense to adjust it
option_c: Keep going no matter what
option_d: Blame something else for it not working
explanation: Persistence is important. But so is reading when something needs to change rather than just more effort. Deciding honestly whether a goal still makes sense is not quitting -- it is being clear-eyed.
hint: Is continuing always the right answer?

question: You reached a goal you had been working toward for months. You expected to feel great. Instead you feel a bit flat. Why might that be?
option_a: You set the wrong goal
option_b: The feeling after reaching a goal often fades faster than you expect -- the next goal matters as much as this one
option_c: You did not deserve it
option_d: You are just not a happy person
explanation: The feeling of achieving something real is genuine but brief for most people. The flat feeling after is not a sign something is wrong -- it is a signal that the doing mattered more than the having. The next thing is already waiting.
hint: Is reaching a goal the end or a step?

question: Your group is doing something and you are the weakest at it. Everyone knows it. What is the most useful thing you can do?
option_a: Pretend you do not care
option_b: Step back and let everyone else do it
option_c: Be honest about where you are and ask what you can do to help
option_d: Fake it until someone notices
explanation: Being the least experienced at something in a group is uncomfortable. Being honest about it and asking where you can contribute is not weakness -- it is the fastest way to become useful and to actually improve.
hint: Is being the least experienced at something permanent?

question: Your group cannot agree on what game to play. Everyone is arguing. You are the one people usually listen to. What do you do?
option_a: Pick your favourite and tell everyone that is what you are doing
option_b: Say -- right, we have five minutes, let's just do this one and swap later
option_c: Keep arguing too
option_d: Tell everyone to sort it out themselves
explanation: When a group is stuck in a loop, someone making a decision -- even an imperfect one -- moves things forward. Giving it a time limit and offering to swap means nobody feels like they lost.
hint: Does making a decision for the group mean you always get your own way?

question: You are doing a group task and one person is not pulling their weight. Everyone is quietly annoyed but nobody is saying anything. What do you do?
option_a: Do their bit for them so it gets done
option_b: Stay annoyed and say nothing
option_c: Quietly ask them if everything is okay and if they can help with a specific small thing
option_d: Tell the teacher
explanation: Someone not contributing is not always about laziness. Asking quietly if they are okay and giving them one specific small thing to do is more likely to get them involved than ignoring it or making it a big moment.
hint: Is someone not contributing always about not wanting to?

question: Your group has decided to present your project. Nobody wants to go first. You do not particularly want to either. What do you do?
option_a: Wait in silence until someone breaks
option_b: Volunteer to go first -- you can handle it and it helps everyone else
option_c: Suggest doing it alphabetically by name
option_d: Refuse like everyone else
explanation: Going first is uncomfortable. But if you can handle the discomfort, doing it means the group moves and everyone else relaxes. That is a quiet form of leadership -- not about being confident, just about being willing.
hint: Is volunteering to go first brave or just useful?

question: You noticed your group is about to present something that has a mistake in it. There is still time to fix it. What do you do?
option_a: Say nothing -- it is probably fine
option_b: Wait until after and mention it then
option_c: Say -- actually, I think we have a mistake here, can we just check this bit
option_d: Let someone else notice it
explanation: Saying something uncomfortable when there is still time to fix it is more useful than staying quiet. You are not criticising the group -- you are helping them not be embarrassed.
hint: Is it better to notice a mistake before or after?

question: You are the one in your group people look to. You are having a bad day and you are not feeling it. What do you do?
option_a: Let your mood set the tone for everyone
option_b: Push through and act fine all day
option_c: Be honest with the group that you are not at your best today and ask them to step up a bit
option_d: Give up on the task
explanation: Leaders have bad days too. Being honest about it and asking the group to pick up some slack is more useful than pretending. It also tells the group that honesty is okay -- which makes the whole group work better.
hint: Does being the leader mean pretending everything is fine?

question: Your group is heading in the wrong direction on a task and the person who suggested it is very sure they are right. You can see the problem. What do you do?
option_a: Go along with it -- they seem confident
option_b: Say nothing and hope it works out
option_c: Say -- I think I see a problem with this, can I show you what I mean
option_d: Take over and redirect everyone
explanation: Challenging someone who seems sure of themselves is uncomfortable. Framing it as here is what I see rather than you are wrong keeps it from becoming a fight and gives them a way to change direction without losing face.
hint: Is pointing out a problem the same as attacking the person?

question: Something went wrong with a group task and a younger pupil is getting blamed for it even though it was not really their fault. You know the full story. What do you do?
option_a: Stay out of it -- not your problem
option_b: Agree with the group -- it is easier
option_c: Say clearly -- actually that is not what happened, here is what did
option_d: Wait and tell a teacher later
explanation: Standing up for someone who is being blamed unfairly is harder when the group is against them. Saying clearly what actually happened is not taking sides -- it is just telling the truth.
hint: Is telling the truth about what happened the same as taking sides?

question: You are in a group and the task is nearly done. One person has done almost all the work and is not saying anything about it. What do you do?
option_a: Nothing -- they did not complain
option_b: Take some of the credit anyway since you were there
option_c: Name what they did in front of the group -- say this was mostly their work
option_d: Tell the teacher privately
explanation: Giving someone credit they deserve when they are not asking for it is one of the most straightforward decent things you can do. It costs you nothing and means something to them.
hint: Is giving someone credit for their work important even if they do not ask for it?

question: Your group has just been told your project is the best in the year. Everyone is celebrating. You know one person did most of the work. What do you say?
option_a: Celebrate with everyone -- you were part of the group
option_b: Say to the group -- just to say, most of this was their work
option_c: Say nothing -- you did not want to make it awkward
option_d: Take your share of the praise and move on
explanation: A public moment of success is the best time to give someone proper credit. Saying it simply -- most of this was their work -- does not take anything away from the group. It adds something.
hint: Can you celebrate as a group and still give one person their credit?

question: You are new to a group and you can already see a better way to do what they are doing. What is the best first move?
option_a: Tell them straight away their way is wrong
option_b: Watch for a bit, then ask a question about it rather than telling them
option_c: Take over the task
option_d: Stay quiet and do it their way forever
explanation: Walking into a group and telling them they are doing it wrong -- even if you are right -- usually makes people defensive. Asking a question first lets them arrive at the better idea themselves, which means they are more likely to use it.
hint: Is being right the same as being listened to?

question: You are leading a small group activity. One person keeps interrupting everyone else. The group is getting frustrated. What do you do?
option_a: Tell them to shut up
option_b: Ignore it and hope it stops
option_c: Say -- let's let everyone finish before we come back to you, and keep going
option_d: Ask the teacher to move them
explanation: Naming the rule without naming the person as a problem keeps things moving and does not embarrass anyone. It also tells the group you are paying attention.
hint: Can you solve a group problem without making one person the villain?

question: You sit down to do your homework and within two minutes your brain is thinking about something completely different. What is the most useful first step?
option_a: Give up and do it later
option_b: Write down the distraction thought so your brain lets it go, then come back to the work
option_c: Check your phone quickly then start
option_d: Wait until you feel ready
explanation: When your brain keeps pulling toward something else it is usually because it is trying to hold onto a thought in case it forgets it. Writing the thought down tells your brain it is safe to let go -- then you can actually focus.
hint: Can writing a distraction down help you focus on something else?

question: Your teacher is explaining something important. You zone out and miss part of it. What do you do?
option_a: Pretend you heard it and hope for the best
option_b: Ask your friend to explain it later
option_c: Put your hand up and ask the teacher to repeat that part
option_d: Copy from whoever is near you
explanation: Missing something and asking for it to be repeated is completely normal. It is far better than missing the information and falling behind. Teachers would rather you ask than pretend.
hint: Is asking a teacher to repeat something a problem or a sensible move?

question: You are trying to focus but the room is noisy and you cannot concentrate. What is the most useful thing to do?
option_a: Just push through it -- concentration should be stronger than noise
option_b: Try moving somewhere quieter or ask if that is possible
option_c: Get annoyed at everyone who is making noise
option_d: Give up on the task
explanation: Some brains are much more affected by noise than others. That is not a weakness -- it is just how that brain works. Finding a quieter spot when you can is a practical fix, not an excuse.
hint: Is struggling to focus in noise the same as struggling to focus at all?

question: You have a long piece of work to do and you cannot get started. You keep doing other small things instead. What is happening?
option_a: You are lazy
option_b: Your brain is avoiding the uncomfortable feeling of not knowing where to start
option_c: You do not care about the work
option_d: You need more sleep
explanation: Avoiding a task by doing smaller easier things is very common -- it is called procrastination and it is about the uncomfortable feeling of starting, not about laziness. The fix is making the first step so small it is almost impossible to avoid.
hint: Is avoiding starting a task the same as not wanting to do it?

question: You finished one part of a task and now you cannot seem to start the next part. You keep stopping between sections. What helps most?
option_a: Take a long break between each section
option_b: Before you stop a section, write the first sentence of the next one so starting is easier
option_c: Reward yourself after each section
option_d: Read the whole task again from the beginning
explanation: Stopping in the middle of something is easier to recover from than stopping at the end of a section. Writing the first line of the next bit before you take a break means your brain already has a way back in.
hint: Does leaving a tiny thread into the next section help you start it?

question: You are in a group discussion and your mind keeps drifting. You miss what is being said and then feel lost. What is the most honest and useful thing to do?
option_a: Nod along and hope nobody asks you anything
option_b: Say -- sorry, can you say that again, I lost track for a second
option_c: Pretend you understand and piece it together later
option_d: Ask your friend to catch you up afterwards
explanation: Admitting you lost track briefly is less costly than staying lost for the rest of the discussion. Most groups are fine with it. Pretending costs more in the long run.
hint: Is admitting you lost track better than staying lost?

question: You work much better in short bursts than long stretches. Some people say you should be able to concentrate for longer. What is true?
option_a: They are right -- you need to build up to longer sessions
option_b: Different brains work differently -- working in short bursts with breaks is a legitimate strategy, not a cop-out
option_c: You probably have a concentration problem
option_d: You should just try harder
explanation: Some brains focus better in short intense bursts than long slow stretches. That is not worse -- it is different. Working with how your brain actually functions instead of against it gets more done, not less.
hint: Is one way of concentrating better than another?

question: You are trying to focus and you keep having the same intrusive thought about something that happened earlier. What usually helps most?
option_a: Push it down and try to ignore it
option_b: Write it down or tell someone -- getting it out of your head usually frees up space to focus
option_c: Watch something to distract yourself
option_d: Give up on focusing for the day
explanation: Intrusive thoughts that keep coming back are usually trying to make sure you do not forget them. Writing them down or talking to someone signals to your brain that the thought has been received -- which often quiets it.
hint: Can getting a thought out of your head help you focus?

question: Your friend finishes tasks in class much faster than you. You are still working when the lesson ends. Does that mean they did better?
option_a: Yes -- faster means better
option_b: Not necessarily -- being thorough takes longer than being quick, and quality matters more than speed for most things
option_c: Yes -- you should try to be quicker
option_d: Maybe -- it depends on the task
explanation: Speed and quality are not the same thing. Someone who finishes fast may have skipped steps. Taking time to do something properly is not falling behind -- it is doing it properly.
hint: Is finishing faster always the same as doing it better?

question: You are in the middle of a task and someone in the group starts a completely different conversation. You lose your thread. What do you do?
option_a: Join the conversation -- you were struggling anyway
option_b: Say -- give me two minutes to finish this thought, then I am with you
option_c: Get frustrated and give up on the task
option_d: Ignore everyone and say nothing
explanation: Losing focus mid-task because of a distraction is frustrating. Asking for two minutes to finish your thought is reasonable and most people respect it. It keeps you from losing work you already did.
hint: Can you ask for a moment to keep your focus without being rude?

question: You are in a group and you are the one who notices when everyone is getting off track. You keep trying to bring people back to the task but it is tiring. What is the right move?
option_a: Give up and go off track too
option_b: Keep pulling everyone back -- that is your job
option_c: Say to the group -- we keep drifting, can we just finish this bit and then talk about the other stuff
option_d: Let someone else notice for a change
explanation: Being the person who keeps a group on track is useful. But naming it to the group and suggesting a clear path is more effective than constantly pulling everyone back one at a time.
hint: Is there a way to get the whole group to refocus rather than doing it alone?

question: You notice that you focus much better at certain times of day than others. Your best focus time is in the morning but most of your homework gets saved for evening. What would help?
option_a: Nothing -- homework is always done in the evening
option_b: Try to do the hardest tasks when your focus is naturally strongest, even if that means changing your routine
option_c: Drink more caffeine in the evening
option_d: Accept that evenings are just harder
explanation: Everyone has a natural focus window. Doing your hardest thinking when your brain is already switched on -- rather than when it is winding down -- means the same task takes less effort and gets done better.
hint: Does when you do something affect how well you do it?

question: You sit down to study and you realise you cannot remember what the lesson was actually about. You were there but you did not really take it in. What helps most?
option_a: Guess and write something down
option_b: Read over your notes or ask someone what the lesson covered before you try to study it
option_c: Give up -- it is too late
option_d: Watch a video about a different topic instead
explanation: Studying something you did not take in during the lesson is very hard because there is nothing solid to build on. Going back to the basics -- notes, a friend, a quick recap -- gives you a foundation to actually work from.
hint: Can you study something you do not remember learning?

question: You have a big project due Friday. It is Monday. What is the most useful thing to do first?
option_a: Worry about it until Wednesday then panic
option_b: Break it into small steps and do the first one today
option_c: Wait and see if the teacher reminds you
option_d: Ask a friend to do it with you so it feels less scary
explanation: Big tasks feel huge when you look at the whole thing at once. The trick is not to look at all of it -- just the first step. One small thing done today means Friday is not a disaster.
hint: Does doing one small thing today make Friday less scary?

question: You said you would bring something in for school tomorrow and you have already forgotten what it was. What do you do?
option_a: Hope it comes back to you in the morning
option_b: Write it down right now before you forget anything else
option_c: Ask your friend at school tomorrow
option_d: Tell the teacher your parents forgot
explanation: Forgetting things happens to everyone. Writing things down the moment you hear them is not about being bad at remembering -- it is about using a system so your brain does not have to hold everything at once.
hint: Is writing something down a sign you are forgetful or just being sensible?

question: Your group has a task to finish in class. Nobody is sure who is doing what. Time is running out. What do you do?
option_a: Wait for the teacher to sort it out
option_b: Do the whole thing yourself so it gets done
option_c: Say quickly -- right, you do this bit, I will do that bit, let's go
option_d: Tell the group they are all being useless
explanation: When a group is stuck, someone quietly saying who does what gets everyone moving. That is not being bossy -- that is being useful.
hint: Is splitting the task quickly the same as taking over?

question: You finish your work early. There are still ten minutes of class left. What is the most useful thing to do?
option_a: Sit and wait for everyone else
option_b: Check your work or quietly get ahead on something else
option_c: Talk to your neighbour since you are done
option_d: Ask the teacher if you can go early
explanation: Finishing early is an advantage. Using that time to check your work or get a head start on something else is making the most of it. Sitting and waiting wastes it.
hint: What can ten extra minutes do if you use them?

question: You have three things due this week. You do not know which to do first. What is the most useful thing to do?
option_a: Do the easiest one first to warm up
option_b: Do the one due soonest or the one that takes longest first
option_c: Do your favourite one and come back to the rest
option_d: Ask your parents which one matters most
explanation: Doing the thing due soonest stops you running out of time. Doing the longest one first means you are not rushing it at the end. Both are better than doing the easiest thing first and leaving the hard stuff to pile up.
hint: Does the order you do tasks in matter?

question: You are halfway through a task and you realise you do not have what you need to finish it. What should you have done differently?
option_a: Nothing -- you could not have known
option_b: Check what you needed before you started
option_c: Asked someone else to do it
option_d: Started a different task instead
explanation: Checking what you need before you start is part of planning. Finding out halfway through costs more time than finding out at the beginning. Reading the task through once before starting is a habit that saves a lot of frustration.
hint: Does checking what you need before you start save time or waste it?

question: Your group keeps going back and changing things you already finished. The task is not getting done. What is happening?
option_a: The task is too hard
option_b: The group does not have a clear plan -- they are redoing instead of progressing
option_c: Everyone is trying too hard
option_d: The task instructions are wrong
explanation: Going back and redoing finished parts is a sign there was no clear plan for what done looks like. Agreeing on what good enough means before you start stops the group going in circles.
hint: Does having a clear plan stop a group going backwards?

question: You have been given a task with several steps. You skip straight to the last step because it seems more interesting. What usually happens?
option_a: It works out fine -- the last step is the most important
option_b: The earlier steps were there for a reason and skipping them usually creates problems further on
option_c: You get it done faster and that is fine
option_d: Nothing -- steps are just suggestions
explanation: Steps in a task usually build on each other. Skipping to the interesting part without the groundwork is like building the roof before the walls. It can look fine briefly but it is not solid.
hint: Are the steps in a task always there for a reason?

question: You are given a task and immediately think of a better way to do it than the instructions say. What do you do?
option_a: Ignore the instructions and do it your way
option_b: Do it the way the instructions say, note your idea, and suggest it afterwards
option_c: Tell the teacher the instructions are wrong
option_d: Do both versions to show yours is better
explanation: Your idea might be better. But ignoring the instructions to prove it creates problems -- you might miss something, and the person who set the task may have reasons you do not know about. Doing it the right way first and suggesting your idea after is how you get both outcomes.
hint: Is having a better idea the same as the instructions being wrong?

question: Your group finished the task but when you look at it, it does not really answer the question you were given. There is still time. What do you do?
option_a: Submit it -- you did the work
option_b: Say to the group -- I think we answered the wrong thing, can we check
option_c: Say nothing and hope the teacher does not notice
option_d: Start over completely
explanation: Finishing a task and finishing the right task are different things. Noticing before you submit and saying something gives the group a chance to fix it. That is more useful than saying nothing or starting from scratch.
hint: Is finishing the task the same as doing the right task?

question: You are leading a group task and one part of it has fallen behind. The rest of the group does not know yet. What do you do?
option_a: Say nothing and try to fix it yourself
option_b: Tell the group clearly what has slipped and what needs to happen now
option_c: Blame the person responsible for that part
option_d: Pretend it is fine
explanation: Hiding a problem in a group task means everyone is working on assumptions that are wrong. Telling the group clearly what has slipped and what needs to happen is uncomfortable but it is the only way to actually fix it.
hint: Is hiding a problem from your group ever the right move?

question: You have planned everything carefully and something unexpected happens that throws the whole plan off. What is the most useful response?
option_a: Panic -- the plan is ruined
option_b: Decide what still works, drop what does not, and adjust
option_c: Stick rigidly to the original plan
option_d: Give up since planning clearly does not work
explanation: Plans change. Something unexpected happening does not mean the planning was wasted -- it means you have a structure to adjust, which is better than having nothing. Deciding quickly what still works and dropping what does not is the skill.
hint: Is a plan only useful if everything goes exactly right?

question: You always forget your PE kit. You have forgotten it again today. What would actually fix this permanently?
option_a: Try harder to remember
option_b: Leave a spare kit in your bag or locker so forgetting does not matter
option_c: Ask your parents to remind you every day
option_d: Accept that you always forget it
explanation: Trying harder to remember is not a system -- it relies on willpower, which runs out. Building a backup into the situation means the problem cannot happen even if you forget. That is a better fix than hoping you will change.
hint: Is trying harder to remember the same as having a system?

question: You are given a group task and nobody wants to do the part that is boring but necessary. What do you do?
option_a: Leave it and hope someone does it
option_b: Do the boring part yourself so the task gets done
option_c: Make the person who said least do it
option_d: Suggest you all take a bit of the boring part each so nobody is stuck with all of it
explanation: Nobody wanting a job does not make it disappear. Splitting it so everyone takes a small piece means it gets done and nobody feels punished. That is a fair solution.
hint: Does sharing the part nobody wants work better than leaving it?

question: Your group has finished a task early. You have ten minutes left. One person wants to improve it, another wants to leave it as it is. You are the tiebreaker. What do you do?
option_a: Leave it -- it is done
option_b: Improve it -- more time means better work
option_c: Ask quickly -- what specifically would we improve and is it worth the time
option_d: Flip a coin
explanation: More time does not always mean better work -- it depends on what you would actually change. Asking what specifically we would improve and whether it is worth it is a better question than just yes or no.
hint: Is more time always the same as a better result?
